answer:
The Pumble Model Context Protocol (MCP) server allows AI agents to interact with your Pumble workspace, enabling tasks like searching messages, managing channels, and executing actions [1]. There are three primary ways to implement this:

Using the official Pumble MCP server: You create an application within your Pumble workspace, copy the required tokens, and configure your AI client (e.g., Claude, Gemini, or Codex) to connect via the provided HTTP URL [1].

Using community-built servers: Options include open-source packages like @shoutkol/pumble-mcp-server (via npm) [2] or more comprehensive versions like pumble-mcp, which offers full SDK-based access to messaging, file operations, and real-time events [3].

Using managed services: Platforms like Vinkius, Pipedream, and viaSocket provide hosted, ready-to-use MCP endpoints that handle infrastructure and authentication for you [4][5][6].
